name: Platform-neutral trust anchors and fallback pathway
consequences: Immediate: A policy baseline for platform-neutral access is codified, and gates for fallback testing are established. Systemic: Platform-dependency exposure declines by 28% (share of authentication signals sourced outside Apple/Google); upfront costs rise by 12% and procurement cycles lengthen—trade-off: greater sovereignty at the expense of near-term agility. Strategic: Denmark gains stronger sovereignty and an enabling path to reframe future identity procurements around platform neutrality.
options: Codify a platform-neutral access policy and pilot a non-production fallback path using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect against a simulated environment while preserving current MitID trust anchors.
Forge a browser-based fallback alternative integrating hardware tokens and open standards within public procurement constraints to decouple from app-store controlled signals.

Launch a self-sovereign identity ecosystem leveraging verifiable credentials and distributed identifiers, anchored by a government-as-a-service platform with pay-per-use pricing to decouple from Apple/Google.
review: Controls centralization vs. platform independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs and timeline slippage.

name: Open standards and procurement reform for identity
consequences: Immediate: Agency-wide adoption of an open-standards reference architecture is released and procurement rules are updated. Systemic: Interoperability readiness increases by 22% (cross-vendor test readiness) and vendor competition improves by 10 percentage points; trade-off: standardization may slow integration with legacy systems and prolong initial procurement cycles. Strategic: Denmark builds durable cross-border interoperability and reduces supplier dependence, enabling smoother EU alignment.
options: Institute a government-wide open-standards reference architecture with mandatory support for FIDO2, WebAuthn, OpenID Connect, and eIDAS trust marks.
Launch a procurement sandbox to pilot platform-neutral modules across agencies with shared cost and performance incentives.
Adopt a cross-border SSI/DID framework based on verifiable credentials, anchored by a joint EU procurement platform with tokenized funding and shared governance to democratize trust anchors.
review: Controls standards-driven openness vs procurement rigidity. Weakness: The options fail to consider coordination across EU member states' standards alignment.

name: Coalition governance and accountability
consequences: Immediate: A multi-stakeholder governance body is formed with defined remit. Systemic: Policy-consensus index increases by 20% but decision tempo slows by 15% due to deliberation; trade-off: increased legitimacy vs slower execution. Strategic: The project gains durable political legitimacy enabling resilient procurement decisions.
options: Formalize a multi-stakeholder oversight body including civil society and privacy advocates to guide procurement and standards.
Establish a resilience-focused policy framework featuring continuity commitments and supplier diversification metrics.
Create a public-private co-ownership model for trust anchors, funded via a shared-services pool and performance-based funding, buffered by DAO-like governance.
review: Controls coalition influence vs bureaucratic inertia. Weakness: The options fail to consider governance legitimacy across public bodies.

name: Demonstrator program as credibility engine
consequences: Immediate: Two non-production demonstrators are delivered, focusing on a mobile-oriented and a browser/hardware-token fallback. Systemic: Stakeholder credibility rises by 30%, and subsequent procurement discussions are more constructive; demonstration costs increase by 25%. Strategic: Demonstration success strengthens the case for platform-neutrality in policy and procurement.
options: Deliver two non-production demonstrators using open standards and a GrapheneOS platform to show fallback viability.
Expand to include a hardware-token fallback path and a browser-based option to broaden compatibility.
Prototype a live, SSI-based demonstrator with verifiable credentials and hardware-backed keys, supported by a shared-pay-per-use testing contract.
review: Controls demonstration credibility vs scope creep. Weakness: The options fail to consider security risk from non-production demonstration.

name: EU alignment and procurement influence
consequences: Immediate: EU-engagement initiated and a mapping of European Digital Identity Wallet requirements completed. Systemic: Cross-border interoperability readiness improves by 20% and domestic procurement timelines lengthen by 15% to match EU alignment. Strategic: Denmark positions itself as a credible EU partner shaping platform-neutrality standards.
options: Engage EU Digital Identity Wallet stakeholders to align Denmark's requirements with European interoperability.
Publish policy proposals and contribute AltID procurement language to embed platform neutrality.
Advance an EU-wide cross-border identity framework anchored in SSI/DID and tokenized funding, creating an Identity-as-a-Service consortium with shared governance.
review: Controls EU alignment vs national procurement sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider domestic regulatory capture risk and compliance overhead.

name: Risk-sharing and funding discipline
consequences: Immediate: New risk-sharing contracts introduced and staged investment gates established. Systemic: Budget volatility decreases by 18% due to risk pooling and external audits, but administrative overhead increases by 12%. Strategic: The project reduces financial risk and improves execution reliability, enabling more aggressive procurement terms.
options: Institute stage-gate decisions with go/no-go milestones and strict budget controls.
Diversify funding streams and establish contingency reserves with independent security review.
Adopt a pooled risk-sharing model with performance-based, tokenized funding and external security-insurance coverage, plus DAO-like governance.
review: Controls risk-sharing funding vs budget discipline. Weakness: The options fail to consider administrative overhead and auditing complexity.

name: Platform-neutral procurement mandates for AltID and fallback pathways
consequences: Immediate: Draft and publish amendments to AltID-related procurement documents to require platform-neutral fallback capabilities and multi-vendor onboarding. Systemic: Platform-dependency risk declines by 25 percentage points (from 60% to 35%), a measurable reduction in exposure to platform control, but procurement complexity increases by 20%, signaling the resilience-cost trade-off between robustness and efficiency. Strategic: Denmark gains long-term autonomy and resilience against platform policy shifts, though the upfront administration and coordination burden will continue to grow during the transition.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into AltID procurement language while preserving the current vendor base.
Pilot a standards-aligned multi-vendor fallback within a defined public-service subset with explicit SLA penalties for cooperation.
Adopt a radical EU-wide procurement framework that requires a certified open-source fallback authentication stack with a tokenized funding model and multi-stakeholder governance.
review: Controls speed of procurement vs. robustness of platform independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider coordination overhead and budgetary constraints of multi-vendor onboarding.

name: Open-source fallback demonstrator and governance framework
consequences: Immediate: Initiate development of a reference open-source platform-neutral fallback API and conduct a small-scale demonstration with three services. Systemic: Interoperability increases by 40 percentage points across tested services, reducing time-to-market for new services by 25%, but maintenance complexity and governance costs rise by 25%. Strategic: The project builds credibility for a non-vendor-locked path and supports EU alignment by proving reusable, standards-based components.
options: Release a reference open-source platform-neutral fallback API and pilot with a small group of services.
Develop a standards-based interoperable testbed with multiple OS vendors and implement a certified fallback path for AltID in three public services.
Create a decentralized identity network with tokenized governance and a revenue model to sustain OpenID Connect + WebAuthn fallback across the EU.
review: Controls openness and innovation vs. security certification costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider the governance burden and maintenance costs of an open-source fallback framework.

name: Resilience-focused coalition-building and governance
consequences: Immediate: Form a formal coalition with privacy advocates and civil society to oversee the fallback plan and publish a resilience framework. Systemic: Governance coverage expands transparency and resilience score improves by 30% in annual assessment, but decision cycles lengthen by 15–20% due to multi-stakeholder deliberation. Strategic: This builds legitimacy and reduces political risk, though it slows decisive action and may elongate timelines.
options: Form a formal coalition with privacy advocates and civil society to oversee fallback planning and procurement.
Establish a cross-agency resilience review board with regular audits and shared risk registers.
Create a trans-government resilience pact and joint fund to finance platform-neutral fallback development.
review: Controls speed of coalition-building vs. legitimacy and interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of scope creep and inter-agency political frictions.

name: EU interoperability lobbying and alignment plan
consequences: Immediate: Publish a position on EU wallet alignment and begin engagement with EU bodies. Systemic: Alignment with EU Wallet standards increases by 25 percentage points (alignment index from 0.35 to 0.60), but regulatory negotiations create upfront transaction costs increasing by 15%. Strategic: Denmark gains long-term interoperability and EU support, at the cost of slower national timelines and greater governance overhead due to EU process.
options: Lobby to align AltID procurement with EU Wallet standards and NSIS, preserving current MitID vendor base.
Lead an EU working group to harmonize NSIS-level platform-neutral fallback requirements and procurement guidance across member states.
Form a cross-border platform-neutral identity wallet consortium with tokenized funding and shared open-source infrastructure.
review: Controls sovereignty vs European alignment. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of slower national decision-making due to EU processes.

name: Alternate trust anchors and hardware-backed attestation strategy
consequences: Immediate: Introduce optional hardware-backed fallback tokens for select services. Systemic: Platform-signal dependence reduces by 30 percentage points (60% to 30%), but hardware lifecycle costs and logistics complexity increase by 15%. Strategic: Improves security posture and resilience against platform governance changes, while adding cost and user friction.
options: Pilot hardware-backed authentication as optional fallback for high-risk services.
Integrate multi-vendor hardware attestation and revocation checks to reduce reliance on platform-provided integrity signals.
Launch a sovereign root-of-trust with a hardware-token marketplace and cross-vendor attestations.
review: Controls security vs. user friction. Weakness: The options fail to consider user adoption barriers and supply-chain risks for hardware tokens.

name: Public narrative and risk-communication strategy
consequences: Immediate: Launch a risk-communication campaign emphasizing continuity and resilience. Systemic: Public support increases by 20 percentage points (from 50% to 70%), but incumbent resistance intensifies and bureaucratic friction rises by 15%. Strategic: Builds legitimacy and reduces risk of backsliding, while requiring robust counter-messaging to counter opposition from entrenched operators.
options: Frame resilience as a continuity-of-service risk mitigation to reduce political pushback.
Coordinate with civil society, Parliament, and public stakeholders for oversight, publishing progress dashboards.
Establish citizen-owned governance with token-based voting rights to oversee platform-neutral strategy.
review: Controls public support vs. incumbent resistance. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential misinformation and lobbying counteractions.

name: AltID resilience through diversified trust anchors and fallback pathways
consequences: Immediate: A formal platform-neutral fallback pathway is introduced into AltID procurement, bridging to non-Apple/Google trust anchors. → Systemic: Supplier concentration risk declines by 25% as two additional vendors participate, but integration complexity rises with 6–12 new interoperability interfaces, increasing unit costs by 8–12%. → Strategic: The effort strengthens resilience and autonomy, but incurs higher short-term procurement overhead and potential rollout delays.
options: Codify a platform-neutral fallback pathway within AltID procurement, mandating multi-vendor attestations and a defined continuity SLA.
Pilot two open-standard fallback routes (FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect) across two public services to quantify availability and user friction.
Deploy a decentralized identity wallet using DIDs/VCs and cross-vendor hardware-backed keys, financed by a Nordic shared-investment mechanism.
review: Controls platform autonomy vs. external platform dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the transition costs and stakeholder resistance in procurement reforms.

name: EU interoperability and platform-neutrality in AltID procurement
consequences: Immediate: Procurement guidelines updated to reflect platform-neutrality, aligning with EU standards. → Systemic: Interoperability readiness index improves by 20% and cross-border deployment speed decreases by 15%, but ongoing governance and compliance costs rise by 5–10%. → Strategic: Denmark positions itself for EU wallet adoption, but sustaining cross-border coordination remains a challenge.
options: Integrate platform-neutrality clauses into AltID procurement guidelines through EU standardization bodies and Danish authorities with minimal friction.
Pilot joint EU–DK interoperability pilots using open standards across border services to demonstrate cross-border resilience.
Collaborate to fund a platform-neutral wallet sandbox using DIDs/VCs with a Nordic shared-investment fund.
review: Controls platform autonomy vs. external platform dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the transition costs and stakeholder resistance in procurement reforms.

name: Public-private coalition for procurement resilience and oversight
consequences: Immediate: Coalition formed, procurement language updated to include platform-neutral triggers. → Systemic: Governance complexity rises by 12%, but vendor diversification improves by 30%, reducing lock-in; cost to govern grows. → Strategic: Long-term resilience and public trust increase, yet multi-stakeholder alignment introduces execution risk.
options: Establish an independent advisory coalition with civil society and academia to scrutinize procurement language and ensure continuity requirements.
Co-develop a cost-benefit dashboard for taxpayers showing resilience gains vs procurement overhead and timeline impacts.
Form a Nordic sovereign-capital vehicle that funds a platform-neutral identity marketplace built on DIDs/VCs with token-based governance and shared revenue streams.
review: Controls platform autonomy vs. external platform dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the transition costs and stakeholder resistance in procurement reforms.

name: Demonstrator strategy for platform-neutral identity proofs
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators validate baseline interoperability across standards. → Systemic: Cross-vendor compatibility increases readiness by 18%, but demonstration scope requires additional budget and risk provisioning (5–8% cost delta). → Strategic: Provides credible evidence to policymakers, yet does not imply immediate deployment; requires continued funding.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators: mobile-oriented FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS and a browser-based fallback path.
Extend to a hardware-token-based fallback pathway and a simulated IdP environment to stress-test cross-vendor attestations.
Prototype a production-grade identity federation using a DID/VC ecosystem with zero-knowledge proofs, under an open-source governance model and subscription-based municipal access.
review: Controls platform autonomy vs. external platform dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the resource constraints and potential vendor pushback in rapid demonstrator expansion.

name: Procurement risk-sharing and funding discipline
consequences: Immediate: Procurement terms updated to include risk-sharing mandates. → Systemic: Lock-in risk reduces by 40% but project administration costs rise by 8–12%. → Strategic: Continuity and resilience improve; governance becomes more complex due to shared financial constructs.
options: Introduce caps on platform-locked procurement costs and require multi-vendor risk-sharing agreements.
Experiment with multi-year, performance-based contracts and shared funding for fallback infrastructure.
Form a Nordic risk-pooling fund using tokenized guarantees and an insurance-like model to accelerate cross-border platform-neutral deployments.
review: Controls platform autonomy vs. external platform dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the transition costs and stakeholder resistance in procurement reforms.

name: Continuity readiness and incident response for AltID ecosystems
consequences: Immediate: Continuity readiness tests flag single points of failure and drive remedial actions. → Systemic: Availability improves by 15% in simulations, but cross-border key management adds complexity and cost (5–9%). → Strategic: Builds long-term resilience and public trust, but increases ongoing operational overhead.
options: Design a formal continuity plan and run quarterly tabletop exercises plus disaster recovery drills.
Implement regular simulated disruption tests for fallback services, measuring availability and MTTR across platforms.
Launch a sovereign offline-online hybrid channel with cross-border recovery keys, threshold cryptography, and post-quantum-ready attestation.
review: Controls platform autonomy vs. external platform dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost and governance of cross-border key management and offline capabilities.
